disease.name: Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT)
disease.note: Thrombosis of deep veins, usually in the lower limb. A common post-surgical complication and significant cause of morbidity. Virchow's triad (stasis, endothelial injury, hypercoagulability) underlies pathogenesis. Post-operative patients, those with malignancy, and prolonged immobility are at highest risk. In Kenya, awareness of DVT and PE is growing but thromboprophylaxis compliance remains variable. Urgent diagnosis with compression ultrasound and anticoagulation prevents pulmonary embolism (PE) — a potentially fatal complication. Wells' score stratifies pre-test probability.
presentingComplaints: Pain in the calf or thigh
Redness or bluish discoloration of the leg
Suddenly the leg became swollen and painful after surgery

severityAssessment.severityLabel: PE/iliofemoral DVT/phlegmasia/recurrent on anticoagulation/malignancy-associated -> SEVERE; distal calf DVT -> NON-SEVERE

history_questions.question: When did the leg swelling start?
Is the swelling in one leg or both?
Do you have pain in your calf or thigh?
Is the leg warm or red?
Have you had any surgery recently (especially orthopedic, abdominal, or pelvic)?
Have you been immobile or bedridden for long periods?
Have you had a long journey (flight, bus, matatu >4 hours) recently?
Do you have a history of DVT or PE before?
Do you have any family history of blood clots?
Are you on any hormonal contraception or HRT?
Are you pregnant or have you recently given birth?
Do you have any known cancer?
Have you lost weight unintentionally (screen for malignancy)?
Do you smoke or use khat/miraa?
Do you have any chest pain, shortness of breath, or coughing up blood?
Are you on any blood thinners (warfarin, apixaban, rivaroxaban)?
Do you have varicose veins?
Have you had a central line or PICC line recently?
Do you have any leg injury or fracture recently?
Have you had any previous episodes of leg swelling that resolved?
Do you have any autoimmune disease (lupus, antiphospholipid syndrome, inflammatory bowel disease)?
Do you have HIV or TB — or any chronic infection?
Do you have sickle cell disease or trait?
Have you had any recent central venous line or dialysis catheter?
Do you have any liver disease (affects protein C/S levels)?
Do you have nephrotic syndrome (protein-losing kidney disease)?
Do you have polycythemia (thick blood) or essential thrombocytosis (high platelets)?
Do you have paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria (PNH)?
Has any family member had blood clots or died suddenly from PE?
Do you know your blood type? (non-O blood types have higher VTE risk)
What type of work do you do — prolonged sitting or standing?
Have you had a long bus or matatu journey (>6 hours) in the past 4 weeks?
Did you have any leg trauma or fracture recently?
Have you had a plaster cast or splint on your leg recently?
Did the swelling start after starting a new medication?
Do you use any herbal remedies or traditional medications?
Have you had any recent transfusions or IV fluids (irritating to veins)?
Do you have any history of pancreatitis or other abdominal inflammation?
Do you feel like you have a fever or have you been feeling unwell generally?
Is the swelling in the whole leg or just below the knee?
Does the leg feel better when you are lying down flat vs sitting up?
Have you ever been told you have 'sticky blood' or required blood thinners before?
Do you have children — were any of your pregnancies complicated by DVT or miscarriage?
Have you had any recent treatment for infertility (IVF, hormonal stimulation)?

examination_findings.finding: Unilateral leg edema — pitting, usually from mid-calf to thigh
Calf tenderness on deep palpation (along deep vein course)
Positive Homan's sign (calf pain on forced dorsiflexion of foot)
Dilated superficial collateral veins on the leg
Warmth of the affected limb compared to contralateral
Erythema or bluish discoloration
Palpable cord (thrombosed vein) — rare, usually superficial thrombophlebitis
Phlegmasia alba dolens: pale, cold, painful leg with absent pulses (massive ileofemoral DVT)
Phlegmasia caerulea dolens: blue/cyanotic, tense, painful leg with compartment syndrome risk
Fever (low-grade, from venous inflammation)
Tachycardia (DVT alone rarely causes this — suspect PE)
Difference in calf circumference >3cm compared to other leg
Moses sign (pain on squeezing calf from side-to-side)
Peripheral cyanosis or venous gangrene (late)
Palpable cord (thrombosed superficial vein) — suggests superficial thrombophlebitis with possible DVT extension
Pratt's sign (pain on squeezing the calf from front to back)
Lowenberg's sign (pain on calf compression with BP cuff at <180 mmHg)
Skin changes of chronic venous insufficiency if previous DVT: pigmentation, lipodermatosclerosis
Breathing rate and oxygen saturation — tachypnoea or hypoxia suggests PE
Chest auscultation — pleural rub, crackles, or clear lung fields (PE can have normal exam)
Cardiovascular exam: tachycardia, raised JVP, loud P2, right ventricular heave (right heart strain from PE)
Abdominal exam: mass compressing iliac vein (malignancy, fibroids, gravid uterus)
Lymph node exam: inguinal lymphadenopathy (suggests malignancy or infection as cause)
Digital rectal exam: pelvic mass or prostatic enlargement causing venous compression
Skin temperature difference between legs — infrared thermometer reveals >1°C difference suggesting inflammation

bedside_scores.name: Wells' DVT Score (Pre-Test Probability)

components.name: Active cancer (treatment within 6 months or palliative)
Paralysis, paresis, or recent plaster immobilization of lower extremity
Recently bedridden >3 days or major surgery within 12 weeks
Localized tenderness along distribution of deep veins
Calf swelling >3cm compared to asymptomatic leg
Pitting edema confined to symptomatic leg
Alternative diagnosis as likely or more likely than DVT

bedside_scores.interpretation: Score ≥2: DVT likely — proceed to compression US; Score <2: DVT unlikely — check D-dimer (if negative, DVT excluded; if positive, compression US)

subtypes.name: Distal (Calf) DVT
subtypes.management: Anticoagulation (DOAC: Rivaroxaban 15mg BD ×3 weeks then 20mg OD, or Apixaban 10mg BD ×7 days then 5mg BD; or LMWH + Warfarin) for 3 months. Compression stockings (class 2). Mobilization. Alternatively: serial US surveillance for extension (if anticoagulation risk is high).

subtypes.name: Proximal (Popliteal/Femoral/Iliac) DVT
subtypes.management: Therapeutic anticoagulation immediately (LMWH SC weight-based or UFH IV). Transition to DOAC (Rivaroxaban/Apixaban) or Warfarin (INR 2-3). Anticoagulation duration: 3-6 months (first episode with identifiable risk factor) or extended (unprovoked, recurrent, cancer). Compression stockings (class 2) to reduce PTS risk. Consider catheter-directed thrombolysis if extensive and low bleeding risk. Assess for underlying malignancy.

subtypes.name: Phlegmasia Caerulea Dolens (Massive Iliofemoral DVT)
subtypes.management: Urgent anticoagulation (IV Heparin). Catheter-directed thrombolysis (CDT) or pharmacomechanical thrombectomy (PMT). Fasciotomy if compartment syndrome (pressure >30 mmHg). IVC filter if contraindication to lytics or failure of CDT. Fluids and supportive care. Amputation if irreversible venous gangrene.

bedside.name: Wells' DVT Score
bedside.indication: Pre-test probability stratification — guides further testing

bedside.name: Calf circumference measurement (10cm below tibial tuberosity)
bedside.indication: Objective measure of swelling — difference >3cm is significant

bedside.name: ECG
bedside.indication: If PE suspected: tachycardia, right heart strain (S1Q3T3)

laboratory.name: D-dimer (high-sensitivity ELISA or immunoturbidimetric)
laboratory.indication: Excellent negative predictive value — if negative and low Wells, DVT excluded. Elevated in many conditions (surgery, pregnancy, cancer, infection).

laboratory.name: Troponin
laboratory.indication: If PE suspected — elevated suggests right heart strain

laboratory.name: Thrombophilia screen (Factor V Leiden, Prothrombin mutation, Protein C/S, Antithrombin, Antiphospholipid antibodies)
laboratory.indication: Consider if unprovoked DVT in young (<50y), recurrent, or family history. Check BEFORE starting anticoagulation if possible.

imaging.name: Venous Duplex Ultrasound (Compression US) — whole leg
imaging.indication: Gold standard — non-compressible vein = DVT. Sensitivity >95% for proximal DVT, 70-80% for distal.

imaging.name: CT Venography or MR Venography
imaging.indication: If US inconclusive, iliac DVT suspected, or assessment for central thrombosis

imaging.name: Venography (contrast)
imaging.indication: Historical gold standard — rarely used now (invasive, contrast, radiation)

imaging.name: CT Pulmonary Angiography (CTPA)
imaging.indication: If PE suspected — urgent if dyspnea, chest pain, hypoxia, tachycardia

imaging.name: V/Q Scan
imaging.indication: Alternative to CTPA if contrast allergy or renal impairment

management.protocolReference: ACCP / NICE Guidelines for Venous Thromboembolism (2023)

resuscitation: If PE suspected: high-flow oxygen, IV access, ECG monitoring
If massive PE with shock: consider thrombolysis (Alteplase 100mg IV over 2h)
conservative: Early mobilization with compression stockings — do NOT bed rest
Compression stockings (class 2, 20-30 mmHg) as soon as possible
anticoagulation: Immediate therapeutic anticoagulation (same day, once diagnosis confirmed or high suspicion)
DOAC first-line: Rivaroxaban 15mg BD × 21 days then 20mg OD; or Apixaban 10mg BD × 7 days then 5mg BD
Alternative: LMWH (Enoxaparin 1.5mg/kg OD or 1mg/kg BD) + Warfarin (INR 2-3) for 5 days minimum overlap
For cancer-associated DVT: LMWH monotherapy (Dalteparin 200 IU/kg OD × 1 month then 150 IU/kg OD) or DOAC (Edoxaban, Rivaroxaban after initial LMWH)
Duration: 3 months for provoked DVT; 6+ months for unprovoked; indefinite for recurrent or cancer-associated

surgical: Catheter-directed thrombolysis (CDT): for extensive proximal DVT (<14 days, low bleeding risk, good functional status)
Pharmacomechanical thrombectomy (PMT): AngioJet or Trellis device for rapid thrombus removal
Venous thrombectomy (open): rarely indicated — for phlegmasia not responding to CDT
IVC filter: if anticoagulation contraindicated (bleeding, recent surgery with high bleeding risk), or failure of anticoagulation (recurrent PE despite therapeutic AC)
nonOperative: Medical management with anticoagulation is the standard for most DVT

immediate: For CDT/PMT: bed rest with leg elevated for 4-6h post-procedure
Monitor access site (popliteal or jugular vein) for bleeding
day1_3: Monitor leg circumference — should be decreasing
day3_7: Workup for underlying cause if unprovoked (malignancy screening: CT, tumor markers, age-appropriate cancer screening)
day7_14: Transition from LMWH to oral anticoagulation (if Warfarin — INR 2-3 for 2 consecutive days)

pathways.criteria: calf DVT only, below popliteal vein, Wells low-intermediate
pathways.treatment: Anticoagulation for 3 months (DOAC or LMWH + Warfarin). Compression stockings. Mobilization. Serial US if high bleeding risk to detect proximal extension.

pathways.criteria: popliteal, femoral, iliac, or IVC involvement; no phlegmasia or PE
pathways.treatment: Therapeutic anticoagulation (DOAC first-line). Compression stockings. Duration 3-6 months. Consider CDT if extensive, young, low bleeding risk. Assess for malignancy.

pathways.criteria: phlegmasia caerulea/alba dolens, PE with hemodynamic compromise, extensive iliofemoral DVT
pathways.treatment: Urgent CDT or PMT. IV Heparin. Fasciotomy if compartment syndrome. IVC filter if contraindication to lytics. ICU for massive PE. Consider surgical thrombectomy if CDT fails.

operative_templates.indication: Acute extensive proximal DVT (<14 days), low bleeding risk, good functional status with low risk of PTS
operative_templates.approach: Percutaneous venous access (popliteal vein under US guidance or internal jugular vein)
operative_templates.position: Prone (for popliteal approach) or supine (for jugular approach)
operative_templates.incision: Percutaneous access — no incision or 1mm nick
steps: US-guided access to popliteal vein (preferred) or internal jugular vein
Position multi-sidehole infusion catheter within thrombus
Infuse Alteplase 0.5-1.0 mg/h through catheter (total dose typically 20-40mg over 12-24h)
Concomitant IV Heparin 500 IU/h through sidearm
Repeat venogram every 12-24h to assess progress
Remove catheter when flow restored or maximum dose reached
Identify underlying stenosis (May-Thurner, iliac compression) — angioplasty + stent if needed
keyLandmarks: Popliteal vein — posterior to popliteal artery in popliteal fossa
Common femoral vein — below inguinal ligament, medial to artery
Iliac veins — confluence at L4-5 level

dischargeCriteria: Anticoagulation plan established — patient/carer educated on dosing and monitoring
If Warfarin: INR in therapeutic range (2-3) for 2 consecutive days, or bridging plan
If DOAC: patient aware of dose regimen and importance of compliance
Compression stockings fitted and patient educated on wearing daily
No signs of PE (chest pain, dyspnea, hypoxia)
Bleeding risk assessed — no active bleeding
Follow-up plan for completion of anticoagulation, PTS prevention, and underlying cause workup

patient_education.explanation: Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT) is a blood clot that forms in one of the deep veins of your leg. This can cause pain, swelling, and warmth. The biggest concern is that part of the clot can break off and travel to your lungs — this is called a pulmonary embolism (PE) and can be life-threatening. Treatment involves blood thinners (anticoagulation) to stop the clot from growing and to prevent new clots. You will need to take these medications for at least 3 months.
warningSigns: Sudden chest pain, difficulty breathing, or coughing up blood (PE) — go to emergency immediately
Leg swelling increases or becomes blue/purple
Easy or unusual bleeding (gums, nose, dark stools, blood in urine) — may mean medication dose is too high
Severe headache or neurological symptoms (possible intracranial bleed)
Fever with redness or wound issues
activity: Walk daily — gentle activity helps prevent clot extension and recurrence
Wear compression stockings daily (take off at night) for at least 2 years to prevent post-thrombotic syndrome
Avoid prolonged sitting (>2 hours) — stand and walk briefly
Elevate the leg when sitting to reduce swelling
Avoid leg massage or vigorous exercise until clot stabilizes
diet: If on Warfarin: consistent Vitamin K intake (avoid large changes in leafy greens)
No dietary restrictions if on DOAC (Rivaroxaban, Apixaban)
medication: Take blood thinners exactly as prescribed — never miss a dose
If you miss a dose: take it as soon as remembered (unless almost time for next dose)
If on Warfarin: regular INR checks at your nearest lab — target 2.0-3.0
Avoid NSAIDs (ibuprofen, diclofenac) — increases bleeding risk
Carry anticoagulant card/wallet alert at all times
Tell any doctor or dentist you are on blood thinners before any procedure
followUp: Complete the full duration of anticoagulation (at least 3 months)
Follow-up at anticoagulation clinic for INR monitoring (if Warfarin)
Review in vascular clinic at 6 weeks, 3 months, and 6 months
Leg ultrasound may be repeated to document clot resolution
If unprovoked DVT: we will perform screening tests for underlying causes (cancer, thrombophilia)

before_discharge: Anticoagulation education: dosing, monitoring, bleeding warnings
Compression stockings — measure and fit correctly
Screen for malignancy if unprovoked DVT (age-appropriate)

after_discharge.action: Clinical review — leg swelling, pain, anticoagulation tolerance
INR check (if Warfarin). DOAC: compliance check.
Repeat compression US — assess clot resolution
Complete course review — decide on continuing or stopping anticoagulation
If continued: reassess. Post-thrombotic syndrome assessment.
Annual review — leg status, PTS symptoms, surveillance for recurrence

when_to_return: Chest pain, difficulty breathing, hemoptysis (possible PE)
Worsening leg swelling, pain, or blue discoloration
Bleeding that does not stop (gums, nose) or dark stools
Any injury to the head while on blood thinners — seek urgent medical attention

pathophysiologyDetail: Virchow's triad (1856) remains the pathophysiological foundation: venous stasis (immobility, paralysis, high venous pressure, dehydration), endothelial injury (trauma, surgery, catheter, inflammation, sepsis), and hypercoagulability (inherited thrombophilia, malignancy, pregnancy, estrogen, antiphospholipid syndrome). DVT requires at least 2 of the 3 elements

Coagulation cascade in DVT: tissue factor exposure (from injured endothelium) → factor VII activation → factor X activation → thrombin generation (factor IIa) → fibrinogen → fibrin. Thrombin also activates factors V, VIII, and XI (amplification loop) and activates platelets via PAR-1 receptors

Platelet contribution: activated platelets release polyphosphate from dense granules — polyphosphate activates factor XII (contact pathway) and enhances factor V activation. This is why aspirin reduces DVT risk by ~30% (but is insufficient alone for treatment)

Natural anticoagulant mechanisms that normally limit thrombosis: antithrombin III (inhibits thrombin, Xa, IXa — enhanced by heparin), protein C/S (inactivate Va and VIIIa), and tissue factor pathway inhibitor (TFPI — inhibits VIIa/TF complex). Thrombophilia arises when these are deficient

Thrombus propagation: thrombus extends proximally along the venous lumen, often detaching at valve sinuses where blood pools. Proximal DVTs (popliteal, femoral, iliac) have a 40-50% risk of embolization to the pulmonary circulation if untreated. Distal DVT (calf veins) has <1% risk of PE alone but 20% risk of proximal extension if untreated

Post-thrombotic syndrome (PTS) develops in 20-50% of DVT patients within 2 years despite anticoagulation. Mechanism: venous valve damage from the inflammatory response to thrombus → venous reflux → venous hypertension → perimalleolar edema → lipodermatosclerosis → venous ulceration. IL-6 and MMP-9 levels predict PTS risk

In Kenya, DVT is underdiagnosed — the Wells score is rarely used systematically, and D-dimer testing (when available) has cost constraints. Many DVTs present only after embolization (as PE) or after PTS has developed. The true incidence is unknown but is estimated to be 30-50 per 100,000 (vs 100-200 in Western populations), although this may reflect underdiagnosis

Pregnancy is a major risk factor in Kenya (where fertility rates are higher): DVT occurs in 0.5-2 per 1,000 pregnancies. The left leg is affected 3x more often than right (left iliac vein compression by the right iliac artery — May-Thurner physiology, worsened by the gravid uterus). Hypercoagulability from gestational rise in estrogen, fibrinogen, and factors VII, VIII, X, and von Willebrand factor

HIV increases DVT risk 2-10x in Kenyan populations (even without AIDS-defining illness): HIV-associated endothelial dysfunction, reduced protein S levels, and chronic inflammation. ART (especially protease inhibitors) further increases risk through metabolic effects

TB in Kenya: a chronic inflammatory state with elevated fibrinogen and D-dimer — hospitalized TB patients have 5-10x increased DVT risk. Immobility during treatment and concurrent HIV co-infection compound the risk

Sickle cell disease (common in Kenya) increases DVT risk through: hemolysis → nitric oxide depletion → endothelial dysfunction. HbSS patients have 2-4x increased DVT risk compared to general population. Chronic hemolysis also causes pulmonary hypertension (a mimic of PE)

Antiphospholipid syndrome (APS) is the most thrombogenic acquired thrombophilia: antibodies against β2-glycoprotein I cause endothelial activation, increased tissue factor expression, and inhibition of natural anticoagulants (protein C, annexin A5). APS increases DVT risk 10-20x. Triple-positive patients (lupus anticoagulant, anti-cardiolipin, anti-β2GPI) have the highest risk — up to 30% recurrence rate per year without adequate anticoagulation. In Kenya, APS is underdiagnosed due to limited availability of antibody testing — consider in all young patients (<50) with unprovoked DVT

May-Thurner syndrome (iliac vein compression syndrome): the right common iliac artery compresses the left common iliac vein against the L5 vertebral body. This causes intimal hypertrophy, venous spur formation, and stasis → 3-5x increased risk of left leg DVT compared to right. Accounts for 20-30% of left-sided iliofemoral DVTs. Peak incidence: young women (20-40 years), especially postpartum. Mechanism: (1) pulsatile arterial compression → venous wall injury → collagen deposition → intimal 'spur'; (2) stasis from luminal narrowing → thrombus formation. Treatment: CDT + iliac vein stenting (12-16 mm self-expanding stent)

Upper extremity DVT (UEDVT) has distinct pathophysiology: (1) primary (Paget-Schroetter syndrome / effort thrombosis) — occurs in young, athletic individuals after vigorous arm activity (weightlifting, throwing, wrestling). Compression of the subclavian vein at the costoclavicular space → intimal injury → thrombosis. (2) secondary (catheter-associated) — most common cause: central venous catheters, PICC lines, pacemaker leads → endothelial injury + stasis + foreign body. UEDVT accounts for 5-10% of all DVTs. PE risk: 10-15% (lower than lower extremity but still significant). In Kenya, UEDVT is increasingly common with expanding use of central lines in ICU and chemotherapy, and in farmers who perform heavy manual labor

Visceral vein thrombosis (portal, mesenteric, splenic, renal, gonadal, cerebral sinus) represents a distinct entity: (1) usually associated with local factors (cirrhosis, pancreatitis, abdominal infection, malignancy) or JAK2-positive myeloproliferative neoplasms (polycythemia vera, essential thrombocythemia); (2) presentation varies by site: portal vein → variceal bleeding, splenomegaly; mesenteric → abdominal pain out of proportion, bowel ischemia; cerebral sinus → severe headache, seizures, stroke. (3) In Kenya, pylephlebitis (septic portal vein thrombosis) from appendicitis or diverticulitis is an important cause. Diagnosis requires a high index of suspicion and venous phase CT imaging. Management: anticoagulation (LMWH then warfarin/DOAC) unless contraindicated by bleeding risk

landmarkTrials.population: 3,449 patients with acute DVT (EINSTEIN-DVT) and 4,833 patients with PE (EINSTEIN-PE) randomized to rivaroxaban (15 mg BID x 3 weeks then 20 mg daily) vs enoxaparin + warfarin
landmarkTrials.finding: Rivaroxaban was non-inferior for recurrent VTE (DVT: 2.1% vs 3.0%, p<0.001 for non-inferiority; PE: 2.1% vs 1.8%). Major bleeding similar (DVT: 0.8% vs 1.2%; PE: 1.1% vs 2.2%). No need for lab monitoring — significant practical advantage
landmarkTrials.impact: Established rivaroxaban as a first-line single-drug therapy for VTE, replacing LMWH + warfarin in most patients
landmarkTrials.reference: Bauersachs R, et al. EINSTEIN-DVT. N Engl J Med. 2010;363(26):2495-2504; Büller HR, et al. EINSTEIN-PE. N Engl J Med. 2012;366(14):1287-1297

landmarkTrials.population: 2,539 patients with acute VTE randomized to dabigatran (150 mg BID) vs warfarin after initial parenteral anticoagulation
landmarkTrials.finding: Dabigatran was non-inferior to warfarin for recurrent VTE (2.4% vs 2.1%, p<0.001). Major bleeding: 1.6% vs 1.9%. Hepatic safety similar
landmarkTrials.impact: Dabigatran was the first DOAC approved for VTE, offering a fixed-dose alternative to warfarin without lab monitoring
landmarkTrials.reference: Schulman S, et al. RE-COVER. N Engl J Med. 2009;361(24):2342-2352

landmarkTrials.trial: ATTRACT Trial (Pharmacomechanical CDT for Proximal DVT)
landmarkTrials.population: 692 patients with acute proximal DVT randomized to pharmacomechanical CDT (PMT) + anticoagulation vs anticoagulation alone
landmarkTrials.finding: No difference in PTS at 24 months (47% vs 48%, p=0.56). PMT reduced moderate-severe PTS (18% vs 24%, p=0.04) but increased major bleeding (1.7% vs 0.3%, p=0.049). No difference in QoL or venous ulceration
landmarkTrials.impact: Routine PMT is not recommended for all proximal DVT. Selected patients with iliofemoral DVT and low bleeding risk may benefit — this remains an area of individualized decision-making
landmarkTrials.reference: Vedantham S, et al. ATTRACT Trial. N Engl J Med. 2017;377(23):2240-2252

landmarkTrials.trial: CAVENT Trial (Catheter-Directed Thrombolysis for Iliofemoral DVT)
landmarkTrials.population: 209 patients with acute iliofemoral DVT randomized to CDT + anticoagulation vs anticoagulation alone
landmarkTrials.finding: CDT reduced PTS at 24 months (41.1% vs 55.6%, p=0.047). Iliac vein patency at 6 months: 65.9% vs 47.4% (p=0.012). Bleeding complications: 3.8% (no difference in major bleeding)
landmarkTrials.impact: In selected patients with iliofemoral DVT (young, low bleeding risk, <14 days symptoms), CDT reduces PTS and improves patency
landmarkTrials.reference: Enden T, et al. CAVENT Trial. Lancet. 2012;379(9810):31-38

landmarkTrials.population: 8,292 patients with acute VTE randomized to edoxaban (60 mg daily) vs warfarin after initial LMWH
landmarkTrials.finding: Edoxaban non-inferior for recurrent VTE (3.2% vs 3.5%). Major bleeding lower (1.4% vs 1.6%, p<0.01 for superiority in clinically relevant bleeding). Benefit consistent across DVT and PE
landmarkTrials.impact: Edoxaban is a once-daily, fixed-dose DOAC with a favorable bleeding profile for VTE. Approved as a third DOAC option
landmarkTrials.reference: Büller HR, et al. HOKUSAI-VTE. N Engl J Med. 2013;369(15):1406-1415

landmarkTrials.trial: WARFASA - Warfarin vs Aspirin for Recurrence After First Unprovoked VTE
landmarkTrials.population: 403 patients with first unprovoked VTE after completing 6-12 months of anticoagulation randomized to aspirin (100 mg) vs placebo
landmarkTrials.finding: Aspirin reduced VTE recurrence by 42% (HR 0.58, 95% CI 0.36-0.93). Absolute reduction: 6.6% vs 11.2% at 2 years. Major bleeding: no difference (1 per group)
landmarkTrials.impact: Aspirin is better than nothing for extended prevention after unprovoked VTE, though with less efficacy than DOACs or warfarin. Now superseded by DOACs but remains relevant where DOACs are unavailable
landmarkTrials.reference: Becattini C, et al. WARFASA. N Engl J Med. 2012;366(21):1959-1967

landmarkTrials.trial: AMPLIFY Trial (Apixaban vs Enoxaparin + Warfarin for Acute VTE)
landmarkTrials.population: 5,395 patients with acute VTE randomized to apixaban (10 mg BID x 7 days then 5 mg BID) vs conventional therapy (enoxaparin + warfarin)
landmarkTrials.finding: Apixaban was non-inferior for recurrent VTE (2.3% vs 2.7%, p<0.001). Major bleeding significantly lower: 0.6% vs 1.8% (p<0.001 — superiority). Clinically relevant non-major bleeding: 3.9% vs 8.0%. All-cause mortality: 1.5% vs 1.9%
landmarkTrials.impact: Apixaban has the best safety profile among DOACs for VTE (lowest major bleeding rate). It is the only DOAC with a single-drug, no-heparin-bridge regimen (like rivaroxaban) and is therefore the most practical DOAC for many settings
landmarkTrials.reference: Agnelli G, et al. AMPLIFY Trial. N Engl J Med. 2013;369(9):799-808

landmarkTrials.trial: EINSTEIN CHOICE Trial (Rivaroxaban vs Aspirin for Extended VTE Prevention)
landmarkTrials.population: 3,365 patients with VTE who completed 6-12 months of initial anticoagulation randomized to rivaroxaban 20 mg daily, rivaroxaban 10 mg daily, or aspirin 100 mg daily
landmarkTrials.finding: Rivaroxaban 20 mg reduced recurrent VTE by 82% vs aspirin (HR 0.18, p<0.001). Rivaroxaban 10 mg reduced recurrence by 74% vs aspirin (HR 0.26, p<0.001). Major bleeding: 0.5% (20 mg), 0.4% (10 mg), 0.3% (aspirin) — not significant
landmarkTrials.impact: Established that even reduced-dose rivaroxaban (10 mg daily) is highly effective for extended VTE prevention with minimal bleeding. Changed guidelines: extended DOAC (full or reduced dose) is preferred over aspirin for extended prevention after unprovoked VTE
landmarkTrials.reference: Weitz JI, et al. EINSTEIN CHOICE. N Engl J Med. 2017;376(12):1155-1164

landmarkTrials.trial: MARINER Trial (Rivaroxaban vs Placebo for Post-Discharge VTE Prevention in Medically Ill Patients)
landmarkTrials.population: 12,019 hospitalized medically ill patients at high VTE risk randomized to rivaroxaban 10 mg daily vs placebo for 35-42 days after discharge
landmarkTrials.finding: No significant reduction in symptomatic VTE (0.83% vs 1.10%, HR 0.76, p=0.11). In a pre-specified analysis: reduced symptomatic DVT (0.18% vs 0.42%, HR 0.44). Major bleeding: similar (0.28% vs 0.15%). No fatal or ICH bleeding
landmarkTrials.impact: Rivaroxaban for post-discharge VTE prophylaxis does not significantly reduce symptomatic VTE in all medically ill patients. However, in patients >75 years, with prior VTE, or with known thrombophilia, there may be a benefit. Guidelines recommend individual risk-benefit assessment
landmarkTrials.reference: Spyropoulos AC, et al. MARINER Trial. N Engl J Med. 2018;379(12):1156-1166

landmarkTrials.trial: CHEST AT10 Guidelines (Antithrombotic Therapy for VTE — 10th Edition)
landmarkTrials.population: Systematic review and meta-analysis of all available RCT data on VTE treatment (15,000+ patients) — updated clinical practice guidelines
landmarkTrials.finding: Key recommendations: (1) DOACs preferred over warfarin for initial and long-term treatment (Grade 2B). (2) For unprovoked DVT: treat with anticoagulation for at least 3 months, then extended therapy if not high bleeding risk. (3) Rivaroxaban and apixaban are recommended as first-line DOACs (no need for LMWH bridge). (4) Cancer-associated VTE: DOAC preferred over LMWH. (5) No role for compression stockings for PTS prevention (SOX Trial)
landmarkTrials.impact: The most comprehensive evidence synthesis for VTE management. Established DOACs as first-line for virtually all VTE indications. Only exception: pregnant women (LMWH only), mechanical heart valves (warfarin), severe renal impairment (individualize), and antiphospholipid syndrome (DOACs may be inferior — warfarin remains standard)
landmarkTrials.reference: Ortel TL, et al. CHEST AT10. Chest. 2021;160(6):e545-e608

evidenceTable.recommendation: Clinical probability assessment using Wells Score (pre-test probability) before any diagnostic testing
evidenceTable.reference: Wells PS, et al. Lancet 1997; NICE Guidelines

evidenceTable.recommendation: D-dimer testing (high sensitivity ELISA) for low/intermediate Wells score patients. Negative D-dimer (<500 ng/mL) rules out DVT without imaging
evidenceTable.reference: Bates SM, et al. CHEST 2018 meta-analysis

evidenceTable.recommendation: Compression venous ultrasound (CUS) with B-mode compression at 2 cm intervals is first-line imaging. Sensitivity 94%, specificity 98% for proximal DVT
evidenceTable.reference: Lensing AW, et al. Ann Intern Med 1999; NICE Guidelines

evidenceTable.recommendation: Anticoagulation should be started immediately in high-probability patients while awaiting diagnostic confirmation
Initial anticoagulation: DOACs (rivaroxaban 15 mg BID x 3 weeks then 20 mg daily OR apixaban 10 mg BID x 7 days then 5 mg BID) are first-line without need for LMWH bridging
Alternative: LMWH (enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg daily or 1 mg/kg BID) bridged to warfarin (INR 2-3) for minimum 5 days overlap when DOACs are contraindicated

Duration of anticoagulation: 3 months for provoked DVT (surgery, trauma, temporary risk factor). Extended for unprovoked (at least 3-6 months, then consider extended). Extended for cancer-associated VTE (DOAC preferred, minimum 6 months or until cancer resolved)
Graduated compression stockings (30-40 mmHg at ankle) are NOT recommended for PTS prevention (SOX Trial showed no benefit), but may help symptomatically
evidenceTable.reference: Kahn SR, et al. SOX Trial. Lancet 2014

evidenceTable.recommendation: Early ambulation with compression is safe and does not increase PE risk in patients with DVT on anticoagulation — bed rest is not necessary
evidenceTable.reference: Trujillo-Santos J, et al. Ann Intern Med 2005; NICE Guidelines

evidenceTable.recommendation: Extended anticoagulation (>3-6 months) with reduced-dose DOAC (rivaroxaban 10 mg daily or apixaban 2.5 mg BID) is recommended after unprovoked DVT to reduce recurrence by 80% without significant increase in major bleeding
Catheter-directed thrombolysis (CDT) should be considered for acute proximal iliofemoral DVT (<14 days) in patients with low bleeding risk and good functional status to reduce PTS severity
evidenceTable.reference: CAVENT Trial, Lancet 2012; ATTRACT Trial, NEJM 2017

evidenceTable.recommendation: DOACs (rivaroxaban, apixaban, edoxaban, dabigatran) are preferred over LMWH + warfarin for cancer-associated VTE (lower recurrence, similar bleeding)
evidenceTable.reference: SELECT-D Trial; HOKUSAI-VTE Cancer; Caravaggio Trial; CHEST AT10 2021

evidenceTable.recommendation: IVC filter placement is indicated when anticoagulation is absolutely contraindicated (active major bleeding, recent CNS hemorrhage) or for recurrent PE despite adequate anticoagulation. Retrievable filters should be removed as soon as anticoagulation becomes safe

For pregnant women with acute DVT: LMWH (enoxaparin 1 mg/kg BID or dalteparin 200 IU/kg daily) is the only safe anticoagulant. Warfarin and DOACs are contraindicated. Continue LMWH throughout pregnancy and for 6 weeks postpartum (minimum 3 months total)
evidenceTable.reference: Bates SM, et al. CHEST 2012; RCOG Guidelines 2023

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm: Unilateral lower extremity pain, swelling, warmth → Assess pre-test probability using Wells Score (DVT): Clinical signs of DVT (+3), alternate diagnosis less likely (+3), heart rate >100 (+1.5), immobilization/surgery within 4 weeks (+1.5), previous DVT (+1.5), hemoptysis (+1), active cancer (+1). Score >2: high probability; 1-2: moderate; <1: low. → Wells Low/Moderate: D-dimer (ELISA). Negative D-dimer (<500): DVT excluded. Positive D-dimer: proceed to imaging. Wells High: skip D-dimer, go directly to imaging. → Imaging: Compression venous ultrasound (CUS). Non-compressible vein = DVT. Scan from common femoral to popliteal. If proximal DVT confirmed: start anticoagulation immediately. Isolated calf DVT: serial CUS in 1 week if untreated (check for proximal extension). → Additional workup: check for unprovoked DVT — consider thrombophilia screen (under 50, family history, recurrent, unusual site): factor V Leiden, prothrombin gene mutation, protein C/S, antithrombin, antiphospholipid antibodies, JAK2 (for MPN). Cancer screen: CT CAP if unprovoked DVT >50 years or with red flags (weight loss, night sweats, anemia, abnormal LFTs). → Treatment: acute phase: DOAC (rivaroxaban or apixaban) OR LMWH + warfarin. Duration: 3 months for provoked; extended for unprovoked. → Prevention of recurrence: compression stockings for symptom relief. Mobilization. Risk factor modification. ● In Kenya: D-dimers may be unavailable — use Wells score and CUS where available. Cost of DOACs is prohibitive for many — LMWH + warfarin is the standard regimen. INR monitoring is a major barrier (access to labs, patient compliance). In pregnancy DVT, LMWH is the only safe option (warfarin/DOACs contraindicated).

managementPearls.pearl: Not every swollen leg is a DVT. The differential includes: ruptured Baker's cyst (extends to calf, history of knee OA, positive 'crescent sign' on US), cellulitis (red, warm, febrile, WBC elevated — start antibiotics), lymphedema (non-pitting, involves dorsum of foot, history of surgery/radiation), venous insufficiency (bilateral, varicose veins, skin changes, improves with elevation), and, in Kenya, consider filariasis (endemic in coastal areas — eosinophilia, lymph node involvement) and TB lymphadenitis (iliac node compression).
managementPearls.evidence: Wells PS, Lancet 1997; Bowker R, Trop Doct 2004

managementPearls.pearl: May-Thurner syndrome: left common iliac vein compressed by the right common iliac artery against the lumbar spine. Presents with extensive left leg DVT in young women. Risk factors: postpartum (5-10x increased), oral contraceptives. If you see a DVT involving the left iliofemoral veins in a young woman, suspect May-Thurner. Treatment: CDT + iliac vein stenting. Failure to recognize leads to recurrent DVT and severe PTS.
managementPearls.evidence: May R, Thurner J. Angiology 1957; Mousa AY, et al. JVS 2013

managementPearls.pearl: DVT in pregnancy requires LMWH (enoxaparin 1 mg/kg BID or 1.5 mg/kg daily) — NOT warfarin or DOACs. Warfarin is teratogenic (nasal hypoplasia, stippled epiphyses) and causes fetal CNS bleeding. DOACs cross the placenta (no data in pregnancy). LMWH is safe — monitor anti-Xa levels for extremes of body weight. Continue LMWH throughout pregnancy and for 6 weeks postpartum (at least 3 months total). Regional anesthesia requires withholding for 24h after treatment-dose LMWH.
managementPearls.evidence: Bates SM, et al. CHEST 2012; RCOG Guidelines

managementPearls.context: The post-thrombotic syndrome — prevention is key
managementPearls.pearl: PTS occurs in 20-50% of DVT patients within 2 years. The only proven preventative intervention is early, effective anticoagulation to prevent thrombus propagation and allow intrinsic thrombolysis. Initiate anticoagulation within 4 hours of diagnosis. For iliofemoral DVT (<14 days, young, low bleeding risk), consider CDT (CAVENT Trial). Graduated stockings are NOT effective for prevention (SOX Trial). Once PTS develops: elevate, compression, wound care for ulcers, rare venous stenting.
managementPearls.evidence: Kahn SR, SOX Trial, Lancet 2014; CAVENT Trial, Lancet 2012; Vedantham S, ATTRACT, NEJM 2017

managementPearls.context: Pulmonary embolism — always think of it
managementPearls.pearl: PE is the killer. 50% of patients with asymptomatic PE at DVT diagnosis — and 10% of proximal DVTs will embolize clinically within days/weeks without treatment. A proximal DVT that has not yet caused dyspnea is a PE waiting to happen. Start anticoagulation immediately. If the patient has dyspnea, chest pain, or hypoxia: this is PE — the DVT is the source but the PE is the emergency. For massive PE (shock): thrombolysis, not just anticoagulation. For submassive (RV strain): consider thrombolysis. Anticoagulation treats both DVT and PE.
managementPearls.evidence: Iles S, et al. Chest 2005; Konstantinides SV, et al. PERT; ESC Guidelines 2019

managementPearls.context: Anticoagulation in renal impairment — choose wisely
managementPearls.pearl: DOAC clearance varies by kidney function: (1) Rivaroxaban: avoid if CrCl <15 mL/min; dose 15 mg daily (not 20 mg) if CrCl 15-29. (2) Apixaban: CrCl >25 — standard dose. CrCl 15-24 — use caution, no dose reduction needed. (3) Edoxaban: avoid if CrCl <15; CrCl 15-50 — standard dose 30 mg daily (not 60). (4) Dabigatran: avoid if CrCl <30 (major renal excretion — accumulates). (5) LMWH: dose by creatinine clearance (short half-life, reversible with protamine — safer choice in severe renal impairment). (6) Warfarin: no renal clearance — safest in ESRD. In Kenya, where CrCl is often not available at the bedside, using apixaban (least renal-dependent) or LMWH with anti-Xa monitoring is sensible.
managementPearls.evidence: Steffel J, et al. DOAC dosing. Eur Heart J 2021; CHEST AT10 2021

managementPearls.context: DVT in infancy and childhood — rare but important
managementPearls.pearl: Pediatric DVT is uncommon but increasing (central lines, chemotherapy, congenital heart disease). Key differences from adult DVT: (1) idiopathic DVT in children often reveals an underlying thrombophilia (protein C/S deficiency, Factor V Leiden). (2) Renal vein thrombosis in neonates (dehydration, sepsis, diabetic mother) — presents with hematuria, flank mass, hypertension. (3) Treatment: LMWH is standard (no RCT data in pediatrics — extrapolated from adults). (4) Duration: 3 months for provoked; 6-12 months for unprovoked; indefinite for recurrent or thrombophilia. (5) DOACs are increasingly used in children (EINSTEIN-Jr — rivaroxaban approved for children from birth). In Kenya: TB-related DVT in children (compression of vessels by TB lymph nodes) is a unique presentation.
managementPearls.evidence: Monagle P, et al. CHEST Guidelines 2012; EINSTEIN-Jr, Büller HR, Lancet 2019

managementPearls.context: Superficial thrombophlebitis — not just a trivial condition
managementPearls.pearl: Superficial thrombophlebitis (STP) is often dismissed as 'just a varicose vein inflammation', but 15-25% extend to DVT if untreated. The STP patient with a tender red cord in the leg needs: (1) ultrasound to confirm STP and exclude DVT (compression of the deep veins — mandatory). (2) If STP involves the GSV above the knee (within 3 cm of the SFJ), there is a 30% risk of DVT extension — treat with LMWH for 4 weeks or consider SFJ ligation. (3) If below-knee STP: NSAIDs + compression, but repeat US in 1 week to check for extension. (4) If recurrent or migratory STP: think of underlying malignancy (Trousseau's syndrome — pancreatic, lung, gastric) or thrombophilia. STP is not 'just inflammation' — it is a thromboembolic condition.
managementPearls.evidence: Di Nisio M, et al. STP and DVT. Lancet Haematol 2021; CHEST Guidelines

managementPearls.context: The tragedy of missed PE — when DVT is the clue
managementPearls.pearl: Every DVT patient with dyspnea, chest pain, or hypoxia has a PE until proven otherwise. The Wells criteria for PE: clinical signs of DVT (3 points), PE as likely or more likely than other diagnosis (3), HR >100 (1.5), immobilization/surgery within 4 weeks (1.5), previous DVT/PE (1.5), hemoptysis (1), cancer (1). Score >6: high probability — start anticoagulation empirically and arrange CTPA. Score 2-6: moderate — D-dimer first. Score <2: low — D-dimer. A negative D-dimer (ELISA <500 ng/mL) in low/moderate probability effectively rules out PE (NPV >99%). In Kenya where CTPA is limited or expensive: use PERC rule (age >50, HR >100, sat <95%, prior DVT/PE, surgery, hemoptysis, estrogen, unilateral leg swelling) — if ALL negative, PE risk is <2% and no further testing needed.
managementPearls.evidence: Wells PS, et al. PE. Ann Intern Med 2001; Kline JA, PERC Rule. J Thromb Haemost 2004

operativeNuances.pitfall: Excessive bleeding from the access site (popliteal vein puncture) — retroperitoneal bleeding from catheter-related vessel injury is often missed
operativeNuances.expertTip: Access the popliteal vein under ultrasound guidance — the vein is posterior to the artery and superficial (1-2 cm deep). Use micro-puncture access (21G needle, 3F micropuncture sheath) to minimize bleeding risk. For thrombolysis: Alteplase at 0.5-1.0 mg/h through a multi-sidehole infusion catheter. Check fibrinogen q6h — if <150 mg/dL, reduce dose. If <100 mg/dL, stop. After CDT, check venogram — if residual stenosis >50%, consider iliac vein stenting. Do NOT stent through fresh thrombus (risk of re-thrombosis).

operativeNuances.pitfall: Stent migration or compression — the iliac vein stenting is prone to compression by the overlying artery, especially if the stent is too short or the lesion is not fully covered
operativeNuances.expertTip: Use a self-expanding venous stent (not a balloon-expandable arterial stent — these collapse in the vein). The stent should extend from the common iliac vein (just below the IVC) across the compression point to the proximal external iliac vein. Predilate the lesion with a 10-12 mm balloon. Post-dilate the stent. After stenting, anticoagulate for 3-6 months (LMWH or DOAC) before aspirin alone.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Placing a filter in a patient who is a candidate for anticoagulation — filter does NOT treat DVT, only prevents PE. Filter thrombosis occurs in 10-20% of long-term filters
operativeNuances.expertTip: Indications for IVC filter: (1) absolute contraindication to anticoagulation (active bleeding, recent stroke/neurosurgery); (2) recurrent PE despite adequate anticoagulation; (3) progression of DVT on therapeutic anticoagulation. Retrievable filters preferred for temporary indications. Attempt filter retrieval within 3-6 months (retrieval success: 90% at 3 months, 50% at 1 year). Complication: filter thrombosis — treat with anticoagulation once contraindication resolves. Do NOT place filters routinely for 'protection' — they should be the exception, not the rule.

operativeNuances.procedure: Pharmacomechanical Thrombectomy (PMT) Using AngioJet or Indigo
operativeNuances.pitfall: Hemoglobinuria and acute kidney injury after AngioJet PMT — caused by contact activation hemolysis ('AngioJet syndrome') — especially with large-volume thrombus (>20 cm) and prolonged activation time
operativeNuances.expertTip: AngioJet works by high-velocity saline jets creating a Venturi effect (Bernoulli's principle) that fragments and aspirates thrombus. To minimize hemolysis: (1) Limit activation time to <300 seconds total. (2) Use the 'power-pulse' mode (infuse 10-20 mg alteplase + heparin into the thrombus through the catheter, let it dwell 15-30 minutes, then aspirate) — the lysis softens the thrombus, reducing the amount of mechanical fragmentation needed. (3) Hydrate the patient aggressively (1L NS pre-procedure, 1-2 L during). (4) Check urine for blood — if hemoglobinuria develops (red/brown urine), give IV NaHCO3 50-100 mEq and continue hydration until clear. (5) Monitor creatinine, K+, hemoglobin next day. Newer devices (Indigo Penumbra, 8F catheter) use continuous vacuum-assisted aspiration without the Venturi effect — no hemolysis risk.

operativeNuances.procedure: Percutaneous Mechanical Thrombectomy of Iliofemoral DVT (Large-Bore Aspiration)
operativeNuances.pitfall: Blood loss during aspiration — large-bore aspiration catheters (12-24F) can remove significant blood volume (100-300 mL per pass) leading to hypotension and anemia
operativeNuances.expertTip: Use a 12-16F sheath (24F for IVC filter thrombus). Technique: advance a stiff guidewire (Amplatz or Lunderquist) through the thrombus. Position the aspiration catheter at the proximal extent of the thrombus. Activate the vacuum (mechanical suction or manual with a large syringe), then slowly withdraw the catheter while rotating (1 cm/s). Aspiration is stopped when no more thrombus is seen in the collection bottle or when flow through the vein is restored. Keep a blood loss log during the procedure. Replace volume with IV crystalloid. If >500 mL blood loss, consider transfusion. Check completion venogram: if residual thrombus >30%, consider additional CDT (overnight lytic infusion) rather than more aspiration.

operativeNuances.procedure: Femoral Vein Cutdown for Iliac Vein Stenting (When Percutaneous Access Fails)
operativeNuances.pitfall: Inability to access the common femoral vein percutaneously in obese patients or those with extensive iliofemoral DVT — repeated needle passes cause hematoma and make subsequent surgery more difficult
operativeNuances.expertTip: When percutaneous access fails after 2-3 attempts: convert to open femoral vein cutdown. Make a 2-3 cm longitudinal incision in the groin over the femoral pulse (the vein is medial to the artery). Blunt dissection through subcutaneous tissue and cribriform fascia. The vein is identified medial and posterior to the artery. Place a 5-0 Prolene purse-string suture on the anterior wall of the common femoral vein. Access the vein through the center of the purse-string with a micropuncture needle (the purse-string controls bleeding). Perform the venogram and intervention. At the end, tie the purse-string to close the venotomy (no need for heparin reversal). This technique is faster and safer than repeated percutaneous attempts that create a 'Swiss cheese' vein.

prognosis.mortality: DVT alone (without PE): 30-day mortality 1-2% (mostly from underlying cancer or other comorbidity). DVT + symptomatic PE: 30-day mortality 5-10% (PE is the direct cause in 2-5%). Massive PE (shock): 30-day mortality 30-50%. Cancer-associated DVT: 6-month mortality 20-50% (cancer progression, not DVT). In Kenya, PE mortality may be higher due to delayed diagnosis and limited ICU access.
prognosis.morbidity: Post-thrombotic syndrome: 20-50% of DVT patients within 2 years. Moderate-severe PTS: 10-15%. Venous ulcers: 3-5% at 5 years. Recurrent DVT: 5-10% at 1 year (anticoagulated), 15-25% at 5 years (unprovoked). PE recurrence: 2-5% despite anticoagulation. Anticoagulation-related bleeding: major bleeding 2-5% in first 3 months (GI, intracranial, retroperitoneal). DVT-related disability: 30% report reduced QoL due to PTS symptoms.
prognosis.survival: 1-year survival (all DVT): 85-90% (excluding cancer-associated: 95%). 5-year survival: 70-80% (mortality is driven by underlying conditions: cancer, cardiovascular disease, age). Unprovoked DVT without cancer: 5-year survival 85-90%. Cancer-associated DVT: 5-year survival 20-30%. At 10 years, 50% of unprovoked DVT patients will have died of unrelated causes — the DVT was a marker of systemic risk rather than the cause.
prognosis.recurrence: Unprovoked DVT: recurrence risk 10-15% at 1 year, 30% at 5 years, 40% at 10 years if anticoagulation is stopped after 3-6 months. Extended DOACs reduce recurrence by 80-90% (at cost of 1-2% annual major bleeding risk). Provoked DVT (surgery/trauma): 1% at 5 years. Cancer-associated: 10-20% per year — requires extended (indefinite) anticoagulation. Thrombophilia: factor V Leiden (heterozygous) 1.5x risk, (homozygous) 3-5x risk; antiphospholipid syndrome: 10-20x risk — indefinite anticoagulation advised.
prognosis.reference: EINSTEIN-DVT/PE, NEJM 2010/2012; RE-COVER, NEJM 2009; CAVENT, Lancet 2012; ATTRACT, NEJM 2017; CHEST Guidelines 2021
